detailed_analysis: Overall the audited surface is small, well-factored, and reasonably defensive. The standout structural risks are concentrated in three places: (1) daemon.py, which is a 37-line loop that silently swallows every exception from sync_once with no error budget, no backoff, and a blocking time.sleep that ignores the stop flag for up to interval_seconds; (2) the lone daemon test, which does not exercise the watch() loop at all — it only pokes _register_signal_if_available, so the cancellation semantics, sleep-while-stopping, and exception swallowing paths are entirely uncovered by tests; (3) portable_archive.import_from_zip, which is 99 lines of decision-then-projection logic that is advertised as transactional (AC-10) but is only transactional for state.json — partial canonical writes and partial render_to_agentic_tool projections are left on disk after a mid-import error, contradicting the docstring. The Windows retry shim is fine functionally (capped attempts, exponential backoff, jitter), but the Windows-specific code paths are not exercised by any test in the audited set, and there is no platform skip marker. archive.py is clean, but archive_move uses retry_fs only for the move itself; the prior _archive_target call which does target_dir.mkdir is not retried. The rules/slash_command IO modules are pure functions over dicts and strings, no I/O, no global state. The main smells there are: parse_rules_md/parse_slash_command_markdown both take 8 parameters and rebuild per_agentic_tool_only/per_agentic_tool_extra by hand in three nearly-identical blocks. The tests are good in shape — real Syncer, real filesystem, no mocking of internals — but test_daemon_signals.py covers exactly one helper, leaving the daemon loop untested. test_portable_archive.py's AC-10 test only verifies state.json was not written — it does not assert that the first canonical (which WAS written before the second failed) has been rolled back.

findings.variant: Catch-all with log-only (silent failure under load)
findings.problem: The daemon catches every Exception from sync_once and continues polling forever with no error counter, no backoff, no circuit breaker, and no way to fail-fast. A persistent IOError (disk full, permission revoked on state.json, broken canonical dir) will spin the loop indefinitely. Given the data-preservation invariant (NFR-01), a daemon that swallows IO errors but keeps running risks masking user data loss.

findings.variant: Cancellation defect — sleep does not observe stop flag
findings.problem: time.sleep(interval_seconds) is unconditional — a SIGTERM/SIGINT arriving in the middle of the sleep is recorded in the stop closure but the process keeps sleeping until the full interval elapses. With a default 30s poll interval shutdown can take up to 30 seconds.

The only daemon test exercises a 4-line helper. The actual watch() loop — SIGTERM handler installation, the swallowed-exception path, the sleep-vs-cancellation race, the stop flag wakeup — has zero coverage.

99 lines covering 4 responsibilities. The docstring claims 'state.json is the last thing written (AC-10 transactional guarantee).' That is only half-true: save_canonical and render_to_agentic_tool both write to disk inside the loop. If the loop fails on decision N, two canonicals and ~8 rendered tool-side files are already on disk with no entry in state.json. On the next sync_once they will be discovered as orphan unmanaged files.

findings.variant: Test name overclaims the property it verifies
findings.problem: The test does NOT assert that the first canonical (saved before the second call raised) has been cleaned up, nor that the tool-side files rendered for the first decision are absent.
Catching BaseException swallows SystemExit and KeyboardInterrupt for the duration of the unlink call.

findings.variant: Best-effort unlink without retry on Windows
findings.problem: Inside import_from_zip on slug displacement, the local canonical file is unlinked directly with no retry_fs envelope. On Windows, a brief AV scanner lock on the canonical JSON would raise PermissionError and abort the entire import mid-loop.

findings.variant: Directory creation not wrapped in retry envelope
findings.problem: archive_move wraps shutil.move in retry_fs but _archive_target's mkdir is called inline before the retry envelope. On Windows, mkdir can hit ERROR_SHARING_VIOLATION if the parent dir is being indexed.

findings.variant: Windows-only branches with no platform-skip on Linux CI
findings.problem: The winerror branch is exercisable only on Windows. The audited test set has zero tests that synthesise an OSError with .winerror=32 to drive that branch.
Eight parameters where three of them (artifact_path, canonical_name, provenance defaulting) overlap in purpose.

findings.variant: Shotgun Surgery — per_agentic_tool_only/extra hand-rolled in 3 places
findings.problem: The same per_agentic_tool_only/per_agentic_tool_extra rebuilding pattern is open-coded three times across rules_io.py and slash_command_io.py.

findings.variant: Inappropriate Intimacy — imports underscore-prefixed symbols from claude_io
findings.problem: slash_command_io.py and rules_io.py both import five-plus underscore-prefixed helpers from claude_io and codex_io. The leading underscore signals 'module-private' but these are de-facto public APIs.
Five parameters — on the threshold but not over. The same five-parameter signature recurs across many parse_slash_command_* and parse_rules_md.

verdict_summary: One critical silent-swallowing finding in the daemon loop, two MAJOR long-function/coverage findings (import_from_zip transactionality, daemon loop is untested), and minor structural duplication across the IO parsers. No security issues.
